title: Rate Limiting and Quotas
description: Protect from overload; enforce per-tenant quotas; supports fairness and cost control.

cost.costNotes: Seeded via heuristics; validate against current pricing and expected workload (throughput, storage, retention, egress). provenance.estimatedMonthlyRangeUsd: Rate limiting can be implemented at multiple levels with varying costs. Min $0 represents application-level rate limiting using open-source libraries (e.g., token bucket in-memory) with no additional infrastructure. Max $500 represents production-grade distributed rate limiting using Redis cluster (~$100-200/month for cache.m5.large) plus API Gateway rate limiting features (AWS API Gateway with throttling ~$100-200/month for moderate traffic) plus observability costs (CloudWatch/Datadog metrics ~$100-200/month). For high-scale scenarios with dedicated rate limiting services like Kong Enterprise or Redis Enterprise clusters, costs could reach $1000-2000/month, but typical implementations stay well under $500.
provenance.adoptionCost: Medium-high complexity implementation. Requires: (1) Understanding rate limiting algorithms (token bucket, leaky bucket, sliding window) - 1-2 days, (2) Implementing or integrating rate limiting middleware/library - 2-3 days, (3) Configuring distributed state management if needed (Redis/Memcached) - 1-2 days, (4) Setting up per-tenant quota tracking and enforcement - 2-3 days, (5) Implementing monitoring, alerting, and observability for rate limit violations - 2-3 days, (6) Testing edge cases and performance under load - 2-3 days, (7) Documentation and team training - 1 day. Total 10-15 developer days at $150/day blended rate = $1500-2250. With AI coding assistants reducing boilerplate by 30-40%, estimate $1500. Complexity stems from distributed coordination, fairness algorithms, and operational monitoring requirements.
provenance.licenseCost: Open-source solutions dominate: application libraries (Bucket4j, Resilience4j, rate-limiter-flexible) are free; infrastructure options include open-source Redis, Nginx/OpenResty rate limiting, Envoy/Istio rate limiting, and Kong Gateway (OSS). Managed API Gateway services (AWS API Gateway, Azure API Management, GCP Cloud Endpoints) include rate limiting in pay-per-use pricing with no separate license. Enterprise rate limiting solutions (Kong Enterprise, Redis Enterprise) exist but are optional upgrades, not required for implementation. Standard implementation uses free/OSS tools.

provides.reasoning: Rate limiting directly provides overload protection by controlling request throughput and preventing system saturation from excessive traffic.
Per-tenant quotas ensure fair resource distribution among tenants, preventing any single tenant from monopolizing system resources.
This pattern explicitly enforces per-tenant quotas as stated in the description, making quota enforcement a core provided capability.
Rate limits and quotas enable billing boundaries and prevent runaway resource consumption costs, as directly mentioned in the pattern description.
Rate limiting patterns implement sliding window or fixed window algorithms to track requests over time periods, providing temporal control over request rates.
Resilience rate-limiting IS the canonical rate-limiting pattern — it enforces per-client, per-tenant, and global request quotas via token bucket or leaky bucket algorithms.

requires.reasoning: To enforce per-tenant quotas, the pattern requires the ability to identify which tenant is making each request through tenant identifiers or keys.
Rate limiting requires tracking request counts and timestamps, necessitating some form of state storage to maintain counters across requests.

supports_nfr.reason: Rate limiting can reject requests under overload, supporting up to four nines availability.
Rate limiting is primarily valuable for protecting high-availability systems; at very low availability targets, simpler approaches may suffice.
Rate limiting adds minimal overhead (typically sub-millisecond), requiring at least 1ms p95.
Under high load or distributed state checks, p99 latency floor is at least 5ms.
Rate limiting state can be restored quickly, achieving RTO of at least 1 minute.
Rate limiting adds infrastructure overhead (state management, latency checks) that may not be justified at very low throughput.
Pattern is GDPR-agnostic; compliance depends on data handling in the application layer, not this pattern.
Pattern is CCPA-agnostic; compliance depends on data handling in the application layer, not this pattern.
Pattern is HIPAA-agnostic; compliance depends on data handling and provider BAAs, not this pattern.
Pattern is SOX-agnostic; compliance depends on audit controls in the application layer, not this pattern.

supports_constraints.reason: Rate limiting is a cloud-agnostic pattern implementable on any infrastructure.
Rate limiting is an architectural pattern implementable in any programming language.
Rate limiting applies to any platform that processes requests or consumes resources.
Rate limiting applies to systems with or without caching layers.
Rate limiting is valuable for async messaging systems to prevent queue saturation but applies universally.
AI inference workloads benefit from rate limiting due to high costs but pattern applies universally.
Multi-tenant applications benefit from per-tenant quotas but rate limiting applies to single-tenant systems too.
Batch processing systems benefit from rate limiting to control resource consumption but pattern applies universally.
Rate limiting protects distributed transaction systems from overload but works with any transaction model.
Real-time streaming systems need rate limiting to prevent overwhelming downstream consumers but pattern applies universally.
Vector search workloads benefit from rate limiting due to computational costs but pattern applies universally.
Rate limiting protects document store systems from overload but applies to all storage types.
Rate limiting protects key-value stores from excessive load but applies to all storage types.
Rate limiting protects graph databases from expensive query overload but applies to all database types.
Rate limiting protects time-series databases from ingestion overload but applies to all database types.
OLTP workloads benefit from rate limiting to protect transactional resources but pattern applies universally.
OLAP workloads benefit from rate limiting to control expensive analytical queries but pattern applies universally.

algorithm.description: Rate limiting algorithm. Token-bucket allows bursts, leaky-bucket smooths traffic, fixed-window is simple but has boundary issues, sliding-window is accurate but more complex.

scope.description: Granularity of rate limiting. Global protects overall system, per-tenant ensures fairness in multi-tenant systems, per-user for individual accountability, per-ip for anonymous traffic.

enforcement_point.description: Where rate limiting is enforced. Gateway (API gateway/load balancer) for centralized control, application for fine-grained logic, distributed (e.g., Redis) for multi-instance coordination.

quota_type.description: What to limit. Requests for API calls, compute-time for expensive operations, data-volume for bandwidth/storage, cost-based for cloud resource consumption.

burst_allowance.description: Whether to allow traffic bursts. Enabled allows temporary spikes, disabled enforces strict rate, limited allows controlled bursts up to a threshold.
